item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a headcannon that loosely ties this "afterlife" to Scientology.

The prisoners speak English, the automatic damage report is in English; Occam's razor would tend to indicate that it originates on Earth. In an Anglophone region to boot. I'm just going to assume this is the crystal servicing the dead of the North American west coast. Perhaps just the Pacific Northwest, there are plenty of sufficiently unpopulated stretches through there that would fit the description. Also take as assumed that there are a great many other crystals throughout other regions of the planet that we don't see because they are still working properly.

Part of the Scientology mythos is that Earth is effectively an intergalactic spirit-prison. The designated dumping ground for those immortal essences that displeased Lord Zenu.

In the normal course of events, recently deceased souls would just flit off to somewhere else in the universe to be reincarnated. That wouldn't make for a very effective prison, now would it?

So Zenu, being a clever stellar overlord, sets up a system that would nab spirits before they got off planet. You can't just put ghosts in a cage, so he'd have to make them want to stay until they could be jammed into a new baby. Perhaps by projecting visions of a very pleasant afterlife, customized on the fly to the individual target.
Once a guard crystal projector reaches capture capacity it proceeds automatically to the nearest re-embodiement center. Wash, rinse, repeat; and you have perpetual thetan containment.

But no system is perfect, entropy always wins. First to break down was the cloaking system that kept the crystals on a higher vibrational plane. Now not only are they forced to take the slow way back to base via only three dimensions, but operating corporally leads to even faster wear and tear.

The second law of thermodynamics is: there's never enough in the budget. So the innovative planetary warden (or crystalline AI or whatever runs this show) borrow an idea from the local prison-industrial complex and has the captured ghosts take care of basic maintenance.

Same as with the chain-gangs that bright idea emulates, an escape attempt is all but inevitable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the implications with this one. The idea of a prison bus that keeps its prisoners in a drugged, blissful "heaven" is really cool to me, and the final note is pretty ominous once you think about it. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the note. It gives a sharp twist to the SCP that prevents it from just being all "aww that's nice" and instead puts it into that emotionally and morally grey area that I love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't make sense to me.

item.title: Death of the Author FTW
item.content:encoded:
It's not prison buses, just transports to the Afterlife.

And the escapee was 682 prior to containment.
And it's well deserved!

item.title: Minor update
item.content:encoded:
The formatting of the final message has been changed to work better with the new site theme.
I also changed the message delivery method from radio transmissions to glass tablets, because why not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, same here. Waiter, make that a +2, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This right here. If it was not for that note, I would have decidedly downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The prisoners are the ones repairing, they are stuck in an unassuming state of bliss. The -As are prisoners, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The prisoners function as the guards and mechanics, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same. I liked the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confused. The implication of the transmission is, I think, that one of the prisoners has escaped and is at large in our reality. However, aren't the -As the guards? They're the ones that repair the prison if it stops moving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Usually there are between 20 and 30 prisoners. I didn't feel like this, or the locations of L1 and L2, is important enough to include in the article.

I would disagree on the first part. I found myself wondering just that. Is it a few statues, a huge cloud of them? It's impossible to know how many are necessary to maintain one of these things if it falls.

That would be my only criticism, though. This was just intriguing enough by the end for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for a good twist. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea, and definitely how it was executed. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A new skip! And it's good! Praise be! We haven't had one of those in… three days?

Shit, this site makes me greedy for other people's work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you author, for ending the mini new skip drought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am one of those people. The note is what led me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, can't please everyone. Without the note some people would've complained that it's too mysterious without real substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They label all the As when they first appear. They reconstructed all the bodies and recovered all the labels, and didn't have any left-over fragments.

Usually there are between 20 and 30 prisoners. I didn't feel like this, or the locations of L1 and L2, is important enough to include in the article.

The language of the transmission is… yeah. I have a believable head-canon for why the phrases in the first addendum are in Earth languages, but the transmission… yeah. Chalk it up to a translation convention or something. Or maybe the originating reality is close enough to ours to have identical languages. That wouldn't be the first time.

Perhaps people there aren't aware of how prisons really work until they get into one. Or maybe they aren't aware of it even after that. Or maybe the low power of discouragement is the reason why they have so damn many prisoners.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it until you actually revealed what it was with that note. It doesn't leave enough to the imagination, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good, but there's a couple minor issues.

SCP-1511-327-A19 was not found among the remains recovered from the scene. The location and physical state of the entity are currently unknown.

How does the Foundation know which one was A19? How do they know it wasn't among the smashed ones?

I do wish you gave a bit more of an idea as to where L1 and L2 are, as well as the typical number of orbiting prisoners. I don't buy that the transmission was translatable (or in an Earth language), but that's not really something you could have done anything about, so I'll let that slide. Lastly, this seems a sufficiently nice prison system that I feel it might lose some of its power of discouragement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup, that's what they are. Stationary prisons (not shown in this article) work the same way.

The afterlives are finely crafted to each prisoner's tastes. There are limitations - no reincarnation or ascending to a higher plane or whatever - but any mundane afterlife you can imagine it will simulate for you. RA3071 can't handle being happy, it would seem.

I actually think that this solution is ethically superior to any real-life prison system, through this is not the point of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually d'awwwing when I read the afterlife phrases. The the radio signal hit me like a brick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are like prison buses, but the prisoners have been altered into thinking they are in some fantastic afterlife?

Interesting, with some cool implications behind it.
